text.title: RAG Evaluation — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the rag-evaluation methodology. Sources: Es et al. — RAGAS framework (arXiv:2309.15217); Shahul et al. — RAG evaluation taxonomy; Pinecone — RAG evaluation best practices.

rule.statement: Require stable document IDs before computing retrieval metrics; if your vector DB returns raw chunks, compute IDs as hash(content).
rule.rationale: Precision@K and Recall@K require matching retrieved doc IDs against relevance labels. Without IDs the calculation is impossible.

rule.statement: Do not conflate RAGAS Faithfulness (grounded in retrieved context) with factual accuracy (correct against world knowledge) — a faithful answer can still be factually wrong if the retrieved context is wrong.

rationale.source: Shahul et al. — RAG evaluation taxonomy

rule.rationale: These measure different failure modes that require different fixes: bad retrieval vs hallucinating generation.

rule.statement: Separate retrieval evaluation from generation evaluation in every run; a combined score masks root cause and misdirects fixes.
rule.rationale: Retrieval and generation have independent failure modes. A high faithfulness score with low hit rate means the model hallucinates consistently from bad retrieval — not a generation problem.

rule.statement: Target these baselines before production: Faithfulness > 0.85, Answer Relevance > 0.90, Hit Rate > 0.90.
rule.rationale: These thresholds represent consistent observed quality in production RAG systems across diverse domains.

rule.statement: Generate synthetic ground truth (question-answer pairs) with a different model than the one being evaluated.
rule.rationale: Using the same model to generate and evaluate creates self-fulfilling evaluations — the evaluated model scores artificially high on its own synthetic questions.
